literal: indication, secondary signification; from √lakṣ 'to mark, to indicate'
invariant_definition: Secondary signification — the word's capacity to refer indirectly when literal reference would be incoherent or contextually inappropriate. Three classical kinds: *jahallakṣaṇā* (the literal meaning is wholly abandoned), *ajahallakṣaṇā* (literal meaning retained, plus the secondary), *jahad-ajahallakṣaṇā* (mixed). The Advaita interpretation of *tat tvam asi* requires *jahad-ajahallakṣaṇā*: the *jīva*-aspects of *tvam* and the *Īśvara*-aspects of *tat* are abandoned, retaining only the shared *cit*-essence. Schools differ on whether *lakṣaṇā* is admissible at this Mahāvākya: Madhva's polemic *Tat tvam asi-Khaṇḍana* attacks the Advaita *lakṣaṇā*-reading.

per_school.definition: *Tat tvam asi* requires *jahad-ajahallakṣaṇā* — the *Īśvara*-attributes (creatorhood, etc.) of *tat* and the *jīva*-attributes (limited cognitive scope) of *tvam* are dropped, with the shared *cit*-essence retained. The construction *bhāga-tyāga-lakṣaṇā* (lakṣaṇā by partial-abandonment) is the technical name.
*Lakṣaṇā* on *tat tvam asi* is rejected. Madhva either parses by sandhi-reanalysis as *atat tvam asi* ('thou art *not* that') or reads *sa* (qualified similarity) — keeping literal meaning intact.

translator_note: 'Secondary signification' or 'figurative reference' acceptable. The technical Mīmāṃsā / Vyākaraṇa apparatus of *abhidhā* / *lakṣaṇā* / *vyañjanā* (the third added in Ānandavardhana's poetics) has no exact Western equivalent in semantics.
